cases.title: The Already-Stopped Seizure
cases.presentation: A 34-year-old man with known epilepsy is found postictal by coworkers after a witnessed generalized seizure that lasted about 90 seconds and has since stopped. He is confused but gradually becoming more oriented over the next few minutes.
cases.question: What is the correct field management?
options: A: Administer a benzodiazepine to stop the seizure.
B: Protect and monitor, confirm glucose is normal, and allow the postictal state to clear while transporting.
C: Restrain the patient to prevent another seizure.
D: Delay transport until the patient is fully back to baseline on scene.
cases.explanation: This is a brief, self-terminated seizure with a normally clearing postictal state in a patient with known epilepsy — no seizure-stopping medication is needed since the seizure has already resolved. Confirming glucose and monitoring for gradual improvement while transporting is appropriate.
cases.clinicalPearl: A seizure that's already over doesn't need to be 'stopped' — confirm glucose, protect, and let the postictal state clear en route.

cases.title: Confusion With a Hidden Cause
cases.presentation: A 52-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is found confused and diaphoretic by her spouse. There is no witnessed seizure. She is oriented to name only.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate first action?
options: A: Assume a primary psychiatric cause and proceed to a mental health facility.
B: Treat the hypoglycemia per protocol, given the low glucose reading and diaphoresis, then reassess mental status.
C: Perform a full neurologic exam before any treatment.
D: Wait to see if she improves spontaneously without intervention.
cases.explanation: Diaphoresis and confusion with a documented glucose of 38 mg/dL point to hypoglycemia as the cause of altered mental status — consistent with the AEIOU-TIPS framework's emphasis on checking and treating glucose early. Treating the hypoglycemia is the priority before further workup.
cases.clinicalPearl: Diaphoresis plus confusion in a diabetic patient is hypoglycemia until proven otherwise — treat first, then reassess.

cases.title: Six Minutes and Counting
cases.presentation: A 45-year-old man is having continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity. Bystanders report it began approximately 6 minutes before EMS arrival, and it is still ongoing.
cases.question: How should this presentation be classified and managed?
options: A: A normal seizure requiring only observation.
B: Status epilepticus — a time-critical emergency requiring airway/oxygen support, glucose check, and escalation per local ALS protocol with rapid transport.
C: A postictal state requiring no intervention.
D: A psychiatric event requiring police involvement rather than EMS treatment.
cases.explanation: Continuous seizure activity exceeding 5 minutes meets the definition of status epilepticus, a time-critical emergency. Field priorities are airway protection, oxygen, a glucose check, and escalation per local ALS protocol (e.g., benzodiazepine per protocol) with rapid transport.
cases.clinicalPearl: Once a seizure crosses the 5-minute mark, stop waiting and start escalating — status epilepticus doesn't resolve itself reliably.
